metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC/test that exercises the `overlap_drain='async'` detached-child path end-to-end: spawn a real child (not mocked), wait for it to exit, then assert the queue has a DONE_ENRICH event and pending count drops to 0 — or document a rationale for deferring this to a named integration test story.
findings.finding: The default production path — `overlap_drain='async'` spawning a detached child process that outlives the parent — is never exercised end-to-end by any named acceptance-criterion test. All proving tests use `--once` (in-process), `always` (synchronous inline), or the no-op gate path; `test_windows_detach_kwargs` only checks subprocess kwargs via mocking, never spawning. The detached child surviving its parent, writing DONE_ENRICH, and pruning the queue is the core new behavior but has no E2E AC.
findings.location: Testing section; AC 'maybe_drain no-ops with no spawn when nothing soaked... detaches a surviving child otherwise'; AC 'rebar enrich --drain processes up to DRAIN_BATCH...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration/end-to-end acceptance criterion that spawns a REAL child process (or subprocess with the actual entry-point) for `overlap_drain="async"` and asserts the ticket reaches DONE state — not just that subprocess was called with the right kwargs.
findings.finding: The DEFAULT `overlap_drain="async"` path — where `maybe_drain` detaches a real subprocess child that imports enrich_drain and runs the drain — is validated ONLY through subprocess mock/kwargs inspection (test_windows_detach_kwargs checks kwargs; test_gate_latency checks no-spawn). No test exercises the full async round-trip: spawn child → child bootstraps correctly (PYTHONPATH, argv) → child claims a ticket → child appends DONE_ENRICH → parent-side queue shows pending==0. The suite goes green while the live `async` default path could be silently broken (wrong argv, missing env, import error in child).
findings.location: Testing section — test_gate_latency, test_windows_detach_kwargs; AC 'maybe_drain … detaches a surviving child otherwise'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test (e.g., `test_async_child_roundtrip`) that: (1) sets up a real store with a soaked ticket, (2) calls `maybe_drain` with `overlap_drain="async"` against a real (non-mocked) subprocess invocation using `subprocess.run([sys.executable, "-m", "rebar._drain_entry", "--drain"])` or equivalent, (3) waits for child exit, (4) asserts the ticket's queue reducer shows no pending entries. Alternatively, add an AC: 'An end-to-end smoke test spawns the drain child against a seeded store and confirms pending drops to 0 (DONE_ENRICH visible in the log) — not merely that subprocess was invoked with correct kwargs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the advisory lock's retirement path: either mandate `flock()` (OS releases on death) rather than `O_CREAT|O_EXCL`, OR add stale-lock detection (e.g. check lock file mtime > `DRAIN_BATCH * per-item-timeout` seconds), OR add an atexit/signal handler that unlinks the lock — and cover this in `test_lock_held_skip`.
findings.finding: The advisory lock `.rebar/enrich-drain.lock` has no defined retirement/cleanup path for the crash case. The plan offers `O_CREAT|O_EXCL` as the primary mechanism, which is NOT released by the OS on process death — a SIGKILL'd or OOM-killed drain child leaves the lock file permanently, silently blocking all future opportunistic drains.
findings.location: Scope section / 'The drain's OWN lenient advisory lock' paragraph / AC: 'Concurrent drain PROCESSES don't double-work'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `O_CREAT|O_EXCL` with `flock(fd, LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)` on an always-present `.rebar/enrich-drain.lock` file (create-if-absent, then flock). The OS releases the flock on any process exit (clean or crashed). The file itself persists harmlessly. This makes stale-lock detection unnecessary and is the standard Unix advisory-lock pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly how the detached drain child receives the tracker/store path (e.g., `rebar enrich --drain --tracker <path>` mirroring push.py's argv[1] convention) and how `rebar enrich status` resolves the store (explicit arg vs. `tracker_dir()` from cwd).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what argument(s) the detached `rebar enrich --drain` child process receives to locate the tracker/store path, nor how `rebar enrich status` discovers the store at CLI invocation time. The existing push.py async precedent explicitly passes `base_path` as argv[1] to the detached child — without the equivalent specification for the drain child, the implementer must guess the convention.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New src/rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py; CLI arm in src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py'; also What section — Detach, POSIX in v1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete module path for the `queue` API (the module that owns `queue.claim`, `queue.reduce_ticket`, and `pending_enrichment`) to the Scope section, e.g. `src/rebar/llm/enrich_queue.py` (S4-owned), so the drainer's import target is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `queue` module — referenced throughout the drainer loop and status command as `queue.claim(...)`, `queue.reduce_ticket(now)`, and `pending_enrichment(now)` — has no declared file path or module name in Scope. A reader cannot locate or implement the interface without inferring it.
findings.location: What section (Drainer loop paragraph); Scope section; Testing (`test_once`, `test_status`, `test_batch_cap`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section add: 'Queue API module: `src/rebar/llm/enrich_queue.py` (owned by S4 / vegan-forgetful-tiger); `enrich_drain.py` imports `from rebar.llm.enrich_queue import pending_enrichment, claim as queue_claim, reduce_ticket as queue_reduce_ticket`.' Confirm the path matches S4's declared output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide and state the advisory lock mechanism (O_CREAT|O_EXCL + explicit unlink-on-exit, OR flock-on-a-persistent-fd released on process close), document the stale-lock recovery behavior, and add an AC/test that verifies a subsequent drain can acquire the lock after a prior drain exits normally.
findings.finding: The advisory lock `.rebar/enrich-drain.lock` lifecycle is unspecified: the plan offers 'O_CREAT|O_EXCL OR equivalent flock with immediate timeout' without deciding which, but these have critically different stale-lock behaviors. O_CREAT|O_EXCL leaves the file on disk after the drain process exits (crashed or normal), permanently disabling all future drains until manual cleanup. fcntl/flock locks auto-release on process exit. An executor must choose one, but the plan defers this decision, and no AC or test verifies that the lock is released after a normal drain run so that a subsequent drain can proceed.
findings.location: What section — 'drain's OWN lenient advisory lock'; Scope — '.rebar/enrich-drain.lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer `fcntl.flock(fd, LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)` on an open fd whose lifetime is the drain process — the OS releases it on process exit (crash or normal), making stale locks impossible. State this in the plan and add `test_lock_released_after_drain` asserting a second drain invocation after a first completes successfully does acquire the lock and processes entries.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `prune()` operation for queue events in Scope: specify whether it deletes the event files (+ `git rm`) or calls a compact variant, which module owns it, and what its call signature is (e.g. `prune_queue_events(tracker, ticket_id, keep=1)`).
findings.finding: The `prune()` call in the drainer loop — 'then `prune` the ticket's queue events so log growth is bounded' — references a function/operation that is not declared in this story's Scope and is not defined in any cited file path. Its implementation (file deletion? a new append? a compact call?) is unspecified.
findings.location: What section (Drainer loop paragraph); Acceptance Criteria last bullet ('The prune step retains exactly the latest queue event per ticket…'); Testing (`test_batch_cap`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'O_CREAT|O_EXCL OR equivalent flock' ambiguity with a commitment to `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)` (matching `_snapshot/janitor.py`'s established non-blocking advisory lock pattern), with the same no-fcntl mkdir fallback for non-POSIX — eliminating the novel O_EXCL mechanism and the OR.
findings.finding: The plan's advisory drain-process lock (`.rebar/enrich-drain.lock`) describes `O_CREAT|O_EXCL` create OR 'equivalent flock with immediate timeout' as alternative mechanisms, but the codebase already has an established, tested non-blocking advisory flock pattern in `_snapshot/janitor.py::run_gc` (fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB), skip-if-held, return) that is the exact analog. Introducing an OR between two mechanisms (O_EXCL create vs. flock) is NIH ambiguity when flock(LOCK_NB) already exists in the codebase and is the codebase's established single-process advisory lock idiom.
findings.location: What section — 'The drain's OWN lenient advisory lock (distinct from the store write lock AND from the optimistic queue-claim)'
findings.suggested_fix: Model the drain advisory lock directly on `_snapshot/janitor.py`'s `run_gc` interlock: `fd = os.open(lock_path, os.O_CREAT|os.O_RDWR, 0o600); try: fcntl.flock(fd, fcntl.LOCK_EX|fcntl.LOCK_NB); except OSError: return 0  # already held`. This reuses the existing idiom, auto-releases on process death (no stale-lock risk), and is consistent with the codebase's other single-process advisory locks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how `overlap_drain`'s detached child interacts with the existing `sync.push` / `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` setting — specifically whether the drain child's `write_and_push` calls (for DONE_ENRICH) should inherit the parent's push policy or always use `always` (like the async push child does), and add a test covering the `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` + `overlap_drain=async` combination.
findings.finding: The plan adds three new LLMConfig fields (`overlap_drain`, `overlap_drain_batch`, `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`) plus their three DEFAULT_* constants. Of these, `overlap_drain` uses the same `off|async|always` enum values that `sync.push` already uses (`_store/push.py:4`, `_push_mode()`). The plan correctly distinguishes the concerns (LLM config vs. sync config), but it does not address whether an `overlap_drain=async` detach should inherit or be coordinated with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`. If the sync push is disabled (off) but drain is async, the drain child will try to push; conversely the drain might unintentionally suppress or double-count interactions. The config-surface addition is justified, but the interaction with the existing `sync.push` toggle is unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/config.py: add overlap_drain (str enum off|async|always, default async)`; What section — `overlap_drain ∈ {off, async, always}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the field names for the two numeric drain settings across all three sections: pick one canonical name (e.g. `overlap_drain_batch` / `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` as the LLMConfig field names, with `DEFAULT_DRAIN_BATCH` / `DEFAULT_DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` as separate module-level defaults), then update the Constants section, Scope section, and all AC references to use those names consistently.
findings.finding: The Constants section names the two numeric settings as `DRAIN_BATCH` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` and says they are 'both on LLMConfig' (implying those are the field names), while the Scope section names the actual LLMConfig fields `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`. The ACs then reference `DRAIN_BATCH` (default 5) — the constant name — not the field name. An implementer reading these three sections would face two conflicting names for the same field and would not know which to use as the actual `LLMConfig` attribute.
findings.location: Constants section vs Scope section (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`) vs AC line 1 (`DRAIN_BATCH (default 5)`)
findings.suggested_fix: Constants section should read: 'Module-level defaults: `DEFAULT_DRAIN_BATCH = 5`, `DEFAULT_DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20`; LLMConfig fields: `overlap_drain_batch` (int), `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` (int).' Update AC line 1 to reference `overlap_drain_batch` (default 5) instead of `DRAIN_BATCH`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-item timeout for `enrich()` calls within the drainer loop (e.g. `DRAIN_ITEM_TIMEOUT_S` constant) and add it to `LLMConfig`; the drainer catches `TimeoutError` alongside other exceptions, releases the claim via lease expiry, logs, and continues — ensuring a hung LLM call cannot hold the advisory lock indefinitely.
findings.finding: No timeout or circuit-breaker is specified for the LLM `enrich()` call within a drain child. If the LLM endpoint hangs (no response, slow connection), the drain child runs indefinitely, holding an advisory lock that blocks all subsequent opportunistic drains for the lifetime of the hung process.
findings.location: What section / 'Drainer loop' paragraph / AC: 'A single enrich failure releases the claim, logs, and the batch continues'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `maybe_drain` wraps the `subprocess.Popen` call in a `try/except Exception` that logs the spawn failure to the drain log (or stderr at DEBUG) and returns without re-raising, so a spawn failure never propagates to the parent write path.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how a `subprocess.Popen` failure (spawn error) is handled in the parent write path. If `maybe_drain` raises an exception from `Popen` (e.g., out of file descriptors, permission denied on the script), it propagates up through `write_and_push` / `push_after_commit`, potentially breaking an otherwise-successful write operation.
findings.location: Scope section / 'maybe_drain injection point (decided)' / `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py::write_and_push`
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich_drain.py::maybe_drain`, wrap the entire spawn block in `try: ... except Exception as e: _log.debug('drain spawn failed: %s', e)` to guarantee the parent's write path is never broken by a drain-side error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `rebar enrich status` returns `{"pending":0,"claimed":0,"soaking":0}` (exit 0) when the store or queue log is absent/empty, and add a `test_status_empty_store` test case.
findings.finding: `rebar enrich status` behavior when the store does not yet exist (first-run, empty repo) is not specified. This is a user-facing command that could raise a `FileNotFoundError` or similar before any events are written.
findings.location: Testing section / `test_status` / AC: 'rebar enrich status prints JSON {"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int}'
findings.suggested_fix: In `cmd_enrich` status handling, catch `StoreNotFoundError` (or equivalent) and print the zero-count JSON, matching the established no-op pattern used elsewhere in the CLI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's justification for placing `overlap_drain` on `LLMConfig`: replace 'VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)' with the accurate rationale (e.g., 'LLM-feature tunables belong on LLMConfig alongside soak/lease settings from S4, not on VerifyConfig which owns workflow-gate booleans for ticket operations').
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'overlap_drain is a STRING enum so it lives on LLMConfig (read via from_env), NOT VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)' — but VerifyConfig is NOT bool-only. Reading _config_schema.py shows VerifyConfig has float fields (verify_window_headroom: float = 0.8) and int fields. The stated justification for placing overlap_drain on LLMConfig is factually wrong, even though the placement conclusion may be correct for other reasons (LLM-feature colocation).
findings.location: What section — 'overlap_drain is a STRING enum (off|async|always) so it lives on LLMConfig (read via from_env), NOT VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical '(which is bool-only via _as_bool)' with '(which governs ticket-workflow gate booleans, not LLM-feature tunables)' to state the real architectural rationale rather than a false codebase claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the `_cli/__init__.py` intercept template: remove the `tracker` argument (consistent with all other intercepts) and have `cmd_enrich` resolve the tracker internally — e.g. `return _llm_commands.cmd_enrich(argv[1:])` — or document how `tracker` is obtained at that call site.
findings.finding: The plan's proposed CLI dispatch intercept signature `_llm_commands.cmd_enrich(argv[1:], tracker)` passes a `tracker` argument, but (a) `tracker` is not in scope at the `main()` intercept point — no other intercept in `__init__.py` resolves or passes a tracker at that layer — and (b) `cmd_enrich` is a new function whose signature the plan implicitly dictates with this call, potentially causing a design inconsistency where `rebar enrich status` would need to resolve the store path internally (as every other intercept does).
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI dispatch intercept in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py — add `if argv and argv[0] == "enrich": return _llm_commands.cmd_enrich(argv[1:], tracker)`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the intercept to `return _llm_commands.cmd_enrich(argv[1:])` (no tracker arg) and have `cmd_enrich` resolve tracker via `config.tracker_dir()` or `_seam.tracker_dir()` internally, consistent with how `_review`, `_workflow`, and `_llm` handle it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` to the Scope edit-target list (the package-data help file that makes `rebar help enrich` work and registers `enrich` in `known_subcommands()`).
findings.finding: The plan does not mention adding `enrich.txt` to `src/rebar/_cli/help/` (the package-data help directory), yet `_help.known_subcommands()` is built from that directory and `rebar help enrich` / `rebar <unknown> --help` resolution paths all consult it. While the `enrich` intercept fires before `_dispatch`, a bare `rebar help enrich` call goes through `_emit_subcommand_help('enrich')` → `_load('enrich')` → returns None → error exit. This is an unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: Scope section (edit targets) and Testing section — `test_cli_dispatch`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the line reference from `push.py:108` to `push.py:112` (the `if mode == 'async':` branch) or `push.py:124` (the `subprocess.Popen` call) to precisely locate the reused detach pattern.
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/_store/push.py:108` as the location of the POSIX async-detach pattern it reuses, but the actual async-detach block in the file starts at line 112 (the `if mode == 'async':` branch) and the `subprocess.Popen(...)` call is at line 124. `push_tickets_branch` itself starts at line 107. While the function is correctly identified, the line number reference is off by 4–16 lines and could mislead implementers trying to locate the exact pattern.
findings.location: Why section: 'rebar ships the POSIX half of this already for async push (`src/rebar/_store/push.py:108`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'src/rebar/_store/push.py:108' to 'src/rebar/_store/push.py:112' (async branch) or cite the Popen at line 124.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the prune step handles ENQUEUE_ENRICH events that are newer than the just-written DONE_ENRICH (e.g., post-prune re-check for any ENQUEUE with HLC > DONE, or limit pruning to events strictly before the DONE's HLC) to prevent silent suppression of concurrent re-certifications.
findings.finding: The prune(keep=1) step after DONE_ENRICH append has a silent re-enrichment suppression race: if a concurrent ENQUEUE_ENRICH (from re-certification on another machine) arrives between the DONE_ENRICH append and the prune execution with an HLC value ≤ DONE's HLC (possible under clock skew within HLC resolution), prune retains DONE as 'latest' and deletes the new ENQUEUE — permanently suppressing re-enrichment for that re-certification until the self-healing fallback scan (cert-without-fresh-digest) eventually fires.
findings.location: What section — Drainer loop, Prune rule (concrete, no 'cycle' ambiguity)
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the prune rule to: 'drop only queue events whose HLC timestamp is STRICTLY LESS THAN the DONE_ENRICH just written; any ENQUEUE_ENRICH with HLC ≥ DONE is preserved.' This ensures a concurrent re-certification's ENQUEUE is never deleted by the prune of a prior DONE.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the file-creation mode for `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` in the plan (e.g., open with `mode=0o600` in `RotatingFileHandler`) so that on multi-user servers the drain log containing potential LLM-traceback output is owner-readable only.
findings.finding: The drain log file `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` has no declared file-creation mode. On multi-user servers (explicitly in the plan's target deployment: 'server/PC/Mac/cloud'), the default umask-dependent mode (commonly 0o644) would make the file world-readable. The drain child's stderr — which includes full Python tracebacks from LLM API exceptions — may contain the API key if the LLM SDK echoes it in an AuthenticationError or logs the Authorization header. A co-tenant on the same server could read the log and extract the key.
findings.location: Scope section / Observability bullet: 'redirects its own stderr there; stdout/stdin are DEVNULL — drain failures stay diagnosable'
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich_drain.py`, instantiate the `RotatingFileHandler` with an explicit `mode` argument: `logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler(log_path, maxBytes=..., backupCount=..., mode=0o600)` — or set `os.umask(0o177)` before creating the log file and restore it. Add this as a one-line requirement in the Observability bullet and the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gate query concretely (e.g. 'a LIMIT-1 scan over queue events with not_before_ns ≤ now and no DONE_ENRICH, returning a bool') and state the complexity bound (e.g. O(1) index lookup or O(queue_events) scan) so the executor can implement and the budget guarantee is meaningful.
findings.finding: The `maybe_drain` gate's 'cheap soaked+unclaimed check' is not specified concretely enough for an executor to implement within the DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS guarantee. The plan says 'cheap is anything soaked+unclaimed?' but does not state whether this is a count query, a boolean EXISTS query, a limit-1 scan, or something else. For large stores, `pending_enrichment(now)` might scan all queue events; without specifying the gate's actual query or complexity bound, the 20ms budget could be violated in practice.
findings.location: Constants section; 'maybe_drain injection point' section; AC 'maybe_drain no-ops with no spawn when nothing soaked, within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS (20 ms)'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'The gate calls `queue.has_pending_enrichment(now)` — a LIMIT-1 boolean query over queue events (returns True on first match) — added to the queue module. This is O(queue_events) worst-case but exits on first hit, making it cheap for the common case (nothing pending). The 20ms budget is validated by test_gate_latency against a store with at least 1000 non-pending queue events to guard against false-cheap fixtures.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing: (a) why maybe_drain is injected into write_and_push and push_after_commit rather than a scheduler, (b) the three distinct locking layers (store write lock / optimistic queue-claim / drain advisory lock) and why each exists, (c) the POSIX-only v1 detach rationale and the authored-but-inactive Windows branch, and (d) the follow-up Windows spin-out.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-cutting architectural pattern — opportunistic detached-drain injection on the write hot path, POSIX/Windows OS-branched detachment, and a drain-level advisory lock distinct from the store write lock and optimistic queue-claim — but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Scope section; maybe_drain injection point section; 'The drain's OWN lenient advisory lock' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/0XXX-opportunistic-drain.md (or a CLAUDE.md section under 'LLM Enrichment') with the key decisions: injection point choice, OS-branching rationale, lock taxonomy, and Windows follow-up. Reference it from enrich_drain.py's module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gate check's complexity or describe an early-exit optimization (e.g., `any(pending_enrichment(now))` short-circuit, or a separate O(1) count-only index) so the 20 ms budget is defensible by design, not just by test fixture.
findings.finding: The gate check's computational complexity on the write hot path is not characterized. The plan asserts a 20 ms budget and references `pending_enrichment(now)` for the gate, but does not bound how that check scales with queue-event volume. If `pending_enrichment` is O(n_events) and events accumulate (e.g., a store with hundreds of tickets each having multiple queue events before prune runs), the gate could exceed the budget at non-trivial but still 'small-scale' store sizes.
findings.location: Constants section ('DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20'); maybe_drain injection point section ('a cheap soaked?-check'); Drainer loop section ('from pending_enrichment(now) PLUS the fallback scan')
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) clarify that the gate uses a short-circuit `any(...)` over the queue reducer so it exits on the first pending ticket (O(k) where k is position of first pending, often 0 or 1), or (b) maintain a lightweight in-memory or sidecar 'has_pending' flag updated on each ENQUEUE_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH append so the gate is truly O(1). Document the chosen approach in the gate's implementation spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion for the prune step: specify what the queue state looks like if the branch is rolled back past a prune commit (with or without the DONE_ENRICH present), confirm the self-healing fallback scan covers the 'ENQUEUE_ENRICH pruned but no DONE_ENRICH on some clone' scenario, and add a `test_prune_rollback_self_heal` (or note in `test_self_heal`) that exercises exactly this partial-state recovery.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE AFTER ROLLBACK for the prune step. The prune physically deletes committed queue-sidecar events (git rm + commit); if an operator rolls back the local branch past a prune commit but not past the preceding DONE_ENRICH commit (or vice versa), the store may be left with a DONE_ENRICH but no ENQUEUE_ENRICH, or with orphaned ENQUEUE_ENRICH events with no corresponding DONE_ENRICH. The plan states prune is 'best-effort' and references the self-healing fallback scan, but never explicitly asserts the post-rollback queue state or confirms the self-healing scan covers the partial-rollback case where ENQUEUE_ENRICH was pruned before DONE_ENRICH existed on the remote.
findings.location: What section — Drainer loop / Prune rule paragraph; also AC: 'Each processed ticket gets a DONE_ENRICH append + a prune of its queue events (bounding growth)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the `rebar enrich` intercept in `_cli/__init__.py` is placed BEFORE the `sub, rest = first, argv[1:]` decomposition (lines ~449+) so that `rebar enrich --help` routes to `cmd_enrich(['--help'])` rather than hitting `_emit_subcommand_help('enrich')` with no `help/enrich.txt` backing it; alternatively, add `help/enrich.txt`.
findings.finding: The `rebar enrich` intercept is placed before the `known_subcommands()` guard (which gates on `help/*.txt` files), which is correct. However, `rebar enrich --help` (invoked as `rebar enrich --help` where `--help` is the first arg after the subcommand) hits the `rest[0] in ('--help', '-h')` branch at line 452, which calls `_emit_subcommand_help('enrich')`, which calls `_help.subcommand_help('enrich')`, which attempts to load `help/enrich.txt` — a file that does not exist in `src/rebar/_cli/help/`. Since the `--help` short-circuit fires BEFORE the intercept check (the intercept checks `argv[0]` but by then `argv[1]` is `--help`), help for `rebar enrich --help` would return `None` / an error rather than the expected usage text. The plan does not mention adding `help/enrich.txt` or overriding this path.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI dispatch intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`'; also `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` lines 451-453 and 436-438
findings.suggested_fix: Place the `if argv and argv[0] == 'enrich': return _llm_commands.cmd_enrich(argv[1:], tracker)` intercept before the `sub, rest = first, argv[1:]` line (mirroring how `review`, `explain`, `workflow` are placed at lines 374-411), so the enrich handler owns its own `--help` parsing — exactly the pattern every other LLM intercept already uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the mechanism by which the LLM API key reaches the detached drain child (e.g., 'child re-reads `LLMConfig.from_env()` at startup using inherited env vars; no key is injected into the subprocess `env=` dict explicitly') so the credential-transport posture on the boundary-crossing path is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare how the LLM API key (from `[agents]/key`) is transported to the detached child process. The child is spawned with `close_fds=True` (which closes inherited file descriptors but NOT environment variables), so if the key is passed via env var it silently flows through; if it is re-read from a config file the child must have filesystem access to that file. The credential-transfer mechanism on the boundary-crossing path is undeclared, making the access posture of the child's key acquisition ambiguous.
findings.location: What section / Detach bullet: 'start_new_session=True, close_fds=True + its PYTHONPATH bootstrap'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Detach bullet: 'The child acquires credentials by calling `LLMConfig.from_env()` at startup, re-reading the same env vars as the parent; no API key is injected into the `subprocess` call's `env=` argument.' This also implies the parent's env (including any `REBAR_LLM_*` vars) is inherited — confirm that is intentional and state it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the What section (under `overlap_drain` enum) that `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` applies ONLY in `async` mode (the cheap soaked-check-then-detach path) and is not enforced in `always` mode (which runs synchronously regardless of latency), so `test_gate_latency` and `test_always_mode` are not contradictory.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain="always"` mode runs the drain synchronously inline, but the plan also asserts `test_gate_latency` enforces `maybe_drain` completes within `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` (20 ms). In `always` mode `maybe_drain` makes a synchronous LLM call (via `enrich`), which will massively exceed 20 ms. The plan provides no protocol for how `test_gate_latency` and `test_always_mode` coexist — their constraints are contradictory and the decision rule for resolving them is absent.
findings.location: What section (`overlap_drain` enum definition); Testing (`test_gate_latency`, `test_always_mode`); Constants section (`DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` gate applies only in `async` mode — it bounds the pre-spawn soaked? check. In `always` mode the drain runs fully synchronously with no time cap; `test_gate_latency` sets `overlap_drain="async"` (or the default).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
